title: Using wood ash in the compost bin and on the garden
subtitle: What wood ash contains, how much to add, and when it raises pH in a way that helps or harms
excerpt: Wood ash from a log fire or wood stove is roughly 25-35% calcium carbonate, 5-10% potassium, and small amounts of phosphorus. It raises soil pH and adds soluble potassium. In a compost bin it is useful in modest quantities; in large amounts it kills beneficial bacteria. On the garden, 50-100 g/m2 per year is the standard agronomic recommendation.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic guidance on wood ash as a garden amendment. RHS soil pH management guidance. Rothamsted Research soil chemistry data on wood ash nutrient content. ADAS fertiliser and manure guide for potassium content of wood ash. WRAP organic waste in the garden composting guide.

content.text: Wood ash from clean wood (logs, pellets, untreated timber offcuts) is a soluble source of calcium, potassium, and trace minerals. The calcium carbonate raises soil pH in the same way as garden lime, though more quickly because it is more soluble. A standard log fire burning through a typical winter produces 5-10 kg of ash, enough to treat 50-100 m2 of garden at the recommended rate of 100 g/m2. Coal ash is not the same: it contains sulphur and heavy metals and should not be applied to garden soil.
Calcium (as carbonate): 25-35% by dry weight. The liming agent; raises pH.
Potassium (as carbonate): 5-10%. Soluble and immediately plant-available. Fruit and root crops benefit most.
Phosphorus: 1-2%. Lower and less available than in bone meal.
Nitrogen: negligible. Wood ash does not supply nitrogen.

A light dusting of ash (one shovel per 200 litres of compost) between layers is beneficial: it neutralises the mild acidity that builds up in a decomposing heap and provides potassium. More than this raises the pH above 7.5, which slows the decomposition bacteria and can drive off nitrogen as ammonia. If your heap smells of ammonia, you have too much alkaline material in it: skip the ash additions for that batch.

Apply in late winter or early spring at 50-100 g/m2 (roughly one handful per square metre). Rake lightly in. Do not apply on waterlogged ground (the potassium washes out quickly) or before rain is forecast for the same day. Keep away from acid-loving plants: blueberries, rhododendrons, azaleas, heathers thrive at pH 4.5-6.0 and even modest ash applications move the pH toward 7, which locks out iron and manganese and causes yellowing.
When not to use wood ash
On alkaline soils (pH above 7.0): further liming is counterproductive and locks out phosphorus and trace elements
On brassica beds in succession (repeated brassica lime is already standard; adding wood ash on top may over-lime)
In a wormery: high pH and soluble salts harm worms; compost the ash separately in an outdoor bin
From treated timber, MDF, chipboard, or painted wood. These can contain formaldehyde, chromium, arsenic, or similar compounds. Do not apply these ashes to food-growing ground.

Store dry ash in a lidded metal bin (it can smoulder if still hot) or a dry plastic bucket. Wet ash loses its potassium rapidly by leaching; keep it dry until application. Use within one winter season if possible.
